actions: Listed among nations under God's judgment to be dispossessed from Canaan

moral: Persistent military aggression against Israel; used as God's instrument of judgment

faithfulness: Made peace with Israel then broke it repeatedly; unreliable covenant partners

truthfulness: Mixed: diplomatic overtures followed by betrayal; Naaman was transparent

actions: Came to aid Hadadezer and were defeated by David, becoming vassal servants

Ben-hadad's forces routed at Aphek, escaping into the city in retreat

moral: Feared as imperial power threatening the temple; executed Jesus and persecuted church

truthfulness: Roman law provided due process; Pilate declared Jesus innocent yet handed him over

actions: Sanhedrin feared Romans would take away their nation if Jesus was not stopped

moral: Many Greeks believed the gospel; their culture sold Jews into slavery (Joel)

truthfulness: Demanded signs and wisdom; some stirred up false accusations against Paul

actions: Sold Jewish captives into slavery to the distant Greeks of Javan

Large numbers of Greeks believed in Antioch when the gospel was preached

moral: Cyrus decreed restoration of Israel; yet the empire enforced rigid, unjust laws

wisdom: Law of Medes and Persians could not be altered — trapped Darius who mourned Daniel

truthfulness: Officials manipulated Darius's rigid laws to trap Daniel through legal deception

actions: Babylon written on the wall: kingdom divided and given to Medes and Persians

Cyrus issued the decree to restore the Jews and rebuild the Jerusalem temple

moral: Used as God's instrument against Babylon; co-administered unjust rigid laws
faithfulness: God stirred them against Babylon as judgment; no covenant relationship with Israel
courage: God called them as warriors who do not regard silver or gold — pure military will
wisdom: Rigid unchangeable law was a systemic flaw; even Darius could not undo it

truthfulness: Their officials used irrevocable law as a tool of legal manipulation against Daniel

actions: Carried Israelites captive to the cities of the Medes under Assyrian deportation

moral: Mixed worship tradition; yet many believed Jesus and showed hospitality to him

wisdom: Samaritan woman recognized Jesus as prophet; many believed on his own word

John.4.39
truthfulness: Samaritan woman was honest about her past when challenged; worship questioned

actions: Excluded from Jesus's initial missionary commission to the twelve
Samaritan village refused Jesus passage because he was going to Jerusalem

moral: Mighty warriors used as divine instruments; themselves subject to God's judgment (Zeph 2:12)

Subdued under the king of the North in the end-time vision; loss of independence
Pronounced as slain by God's sword — judgment on a nation that did not turn to him
